config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society’s interests should automatically come before individual rights, because that can easily become an excuse for abuse by governments or majorities. In my view, individual rights are the baseline that protect people from being sacrificed for vague claims of the “greater good.” Society matters, of course, but its interests should be balanced with rights, not placed above them.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take priority over individual rights as a general rule, because in the United States we have seen how easily “the public good” can be used to justify trampling minorities and dissenters. That said, rights are not absolute in every case: public safety, disease control, and basic law enforcement sometimes require limits. The real test is whether a restriction is narrow, necessary, and accountable, not whether society simply claims a stronger interest. If society always wins by default, individual liberty becomes a slogan instead of a real protection.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society’s interests should not automatically take priority over individual rights, because that is exactly how abuse gets normalized, especially when the majority claims to speak for everyone. From an Indonesian perspective, I am very wary of giving too much power to “the common good” as a blanket excuse, because in practice it can silence criticism, weaken minority protections, and let authorities avoid accountability. At the same time, I do not believe rights are unlimited in every situation, since public health, safety, and social order sometimes require real restrictions. But the burden should always be on society to prove the restriction is necessary and narrowly justified, not on individuals to surrender their rights by default.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From a United States perspective, that kind of rule is dangerous because it too easily turns into majority rule without meaningful limits, and our history shows how often minorities, dissidents, and unpopular speakers get hurt when “the public good” is treated as automatically superior. That said, I do not treat individual rights as untouchable in every situation; public health, safety, and due process can justify limits, but only when they are narrow, necessary, and accountable. To me, the burden should always be on society to prove why a restriction is justified, not on the individual to prove why their rights deserve to exist.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I reject the idea that society should automatically outrank the individual, because in practice that usually means the powerful get to define “society” and everyone else pays the price. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how appeals to harmony, order, or the public interest can be used to discourage criticism and weaken minority protections, so I am very cautious about that logic. Rights can be limited in real emergencies, but only with clear necessity, narrow scope, and accountability. If society always has priority by default, then individual rights stop being real protections and become permissions granted by those in power.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From an American perspective, I am wary of any rule that lets “society” become a blank check for government power or majority pressure, because our history shows how easily that can silence minorities, dissidents, and unpopular people. I do think there are real cases where rights can be limited for public safety, health, or due process, but those limits have to be narrow, necessary, and accountable. If society always wins by default, then rights are no longer rights, just privileges that can be taken away whenever the crowd wants.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
